DSA learning path

DSA foundations and warm-ups

Start with the patterns that dominate coding screens: arrays, strings, linked lists, trees, and the first layer of dynamic programming.

Module progress 0%

0 of 3 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Arrays, hash maps, and two pointers

Practice the fastest path from brute force to linear or near-linear array solutions.

Why this module matters Choose the solving pattern early, then defend the invariant while you code.

Move through the lessons in order so pattern recognition, live coding structure, and company-specific reps build on each other instead of staying disconnected.

Study target 3 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

Data structures and algorithms interview prep 3 lessons 0/3 complete Follow in order for best results Back to DSA roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the prompt shape, pick the target pattern, and name the invariant before typing the first line of code.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
DSA sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Recognize common coding-round patterns quickly from the prompt shape
  • Write a correct first-pass solution before optimizing under follow-up pressure
  • Explain invariants, edge cases, and complexity clearly while coding

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core pattern or invariant in your own words.
  • Walk one example manually, then code the cleanest correct version before optimizing.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear problem restatement, complexity target, and solving pattern.
  • A stable invariant or recurrence that survives interviewer follow-ups.
  • Confident edge-case testing and clean explanation while coding.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/3 complete
Lesson 1 Next
Arrays, hash maps, and two pointers

Practice the fastest path from brute force to linear or near-linear array solutions.

30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Linked lists, binary search, and ordering problems

Move from pointer manipulation into partitioning, ordering, and search-space pruning.

30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Trees, heaps, and intro dynamic programming

Rehearse recursive traversal, state propagation, priority-based selection, and small DP recurrences.

30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Arrays, hash maps, and two pointers

Open

Practice the fastest path from brute force to linear or near-linear array solutions.

Why it matters

A large share of DSA screens begin here. Interviewers want to hear how you choose between indexing, frequency maps, sorting, and pointer movement.

What you will cover

Pattern lensPractice setExecution focus

Good interview signal

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Linked lists, binary search, and ordering problems

Open

Move from pointer manipulation into partitioning, ordering, and search-space pruning.

Why it matters

These questions expose whether you can manage mutable state, midpoint logic, and off-by-one boundaries without getting lost in implementation details.

What you will cover

Pattern lensPractice setExecution focus

Good interview signal

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Trees, heaps, and intro dynamic programming

Open

Rehearse recursive traversal, state propagation, priority-based selection, and small DP recurrences.

Why it matters

Once an interviewer moves beyond arrays, they are often testing whether you can preserve state across recursion and build a concise recurrence without overcomplicating the code.

What you will cover

Pattern lensPractice setExecution focus

Good interview signal

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
30-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
